name: Break-Even Point (units)
description: The sales volume at which total revenue equals total cost and profit is zero: fixed costs divided by the contribution margin (price minus variable cost per unit).

inputs.name: Fixed costs
inputs.description: Costs that do not change with volume over the period: rent, salaries, insurance, equipment.

inputs.name: Variable cost per unit
inputs.description: Costs incurred per unit sold: materials, direct labour, freight, commissions.

outputs.name: Contribution margin
outputs.description: What each unit contributes towards fixed costs: price minus variable cost.

outputs.name: Break-even volume
outputs.description: Units that must be sold for revenue to equal total cost.

expression: CM = P - VC; BE_units = FC / CM; BE_revenue = BE_units x P
assumptions: Price and variable cost per unit are constant across the volume range considered.
Fixed costs really are fixed over the period and volume range.
Everything produced is sold (no inventory build-up between periods).
limitations: Requires P > VC — with zero or negative contribution margin there is no break-even point.
A linear cost model: step-fixed costs, volume discounts, and capacity limits break the straight-line assumption.
Single-product view; for a product mix, use a weighted-average contribution margin.

examples.note: Each unit contributes 25 - 15 = 10 towards 50,000 of fixed costs, so break-even is 5,000 units, or 125,000 of revenue.

citations.quote: A "break even" analysis can be used to indicate the point at which the volume produced and associated revenues equals total cost, and the profit is zero.
The goal is to determine the breakeven point where total revenues and total costs intersect.
